service_overview.what_is_council_tax: Council Tax is a property-based tax charged on domestic properties. It helps fund essential local services provided by Gloucester City Council, Gloucestershire County Council, and the Police and Crime Commissioner. The amount you pay depends on your property's valuation band and who lives there.
what_it_pays_for: Local services from Gloucester City Council (waste collection, street cleaning, parks, leisure facilities)
County Council services (adult social care, children's services, schools, highways, libraries)
Police and Crime Commissioner for Gloucestershire
Parish/Town Council precepts (where applicable - e.g. Quedgeley)

best_practice_guidance.title: Council tax collection: best practice guidance for local authorities
best_practice_guidance.status: Non-statutory guidance. Government declined to replace with statutory code of practice (Select Committee response, February 2024). Councils encouraged to 'have regard' to it.
key_principles: Council tax collection should never come at the cost of fair treatment of residents
Councils should actively identify discounts and exemptions residents may be entitled to — not wait for residents to apply
Frontline staff must have knowledge to give accurate advice on available support
Vulnerability should trigger a different enforcement process — longer timescales, affordability-led recovery
Soft reminders (signposting support) should be considered before statutory notices
Attachments of earnings/benefits preferred over enforcement agents where possible
Enforcement agents must only be used where other recovery methods exhausted
Enforcement agent contracts must include vulnerability assessment standards and referral-back requirements
Liability order costs should be kept as low as possible — residents are already struggling to pay
Some authorities waive liability order fees for CTS claimants or where repayment is agreed
Breathing space scheme gives up to 60 days protection from most enforcement action for those in problem debt
Mental health crisis moratorium: protections last for duration of crisis treatment plus 30 days
best_practice_guidance.vulnerability_toolkit: Cabinet Office Fairness Group Vulnerability Toolkit — https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/debt-management-vulnerability-toolkit
best_practice_guidance.citizen_advice_protocol: Citizens Advice/LGA Council Tax Protocol (2013, updated) — 15 best practice points. Signed by 60-70 councils. Includes: debt advice referrals, vulnerability and hardship policies, Standard Financial Statement for affordability, exempting CTS claimants from bailiff action.

select_committee_and_government_response.committee_report: LUHC Select Committee report on council tax collection, published 1 December 2023

key_clarifications_for_councils.seven_day_rule: The 7-day period between a missed payment and issuing a reminder is a minimum, not a fixed requirement. Councils have discretion to allow longer. Government wrote to all councils confirming this.
key_clarifications_for_councils.full_balance_payable: Where balance becomes payable, councils have discretion to offer affordable repayment plans over a longer period — including more than one financial year. No legal requirement to collect in-year.
key_clarifications_for_councils.12_month_instalment_right: Residents have a legal right to request payment over 12 monthly instalments rather than 10.
key_clarifications_for_councils.imprisonment: Used extremely rarely. No one imprisoned as main offence since 2018. Courts must be satisfied of wilful refusal or culpable neglect — not inability to pay.
key_clarifications_for_councils.enforcement_conduct_board: Government encouraging all councils to use only ECB-accredited enforcement agents. Over 40 firms accredited, covering 95%+ of annual debt enforcement volume.
key_clarifications_for_councils.no_statutory_code: Government declined to introduce statutory code of practice for recovery. Best practice guidance remains non-statutory but is kept under review.

digital_economy_act_data_sharing.overview: Digital Economy Act 2017 powers allow data sharing between DWP, HMRC and councils to identify and reduce council tax debt. Moving to business-as-usual arrangement by 2025.
digital_economy_act_data_sharing.pilots: Two pilot rounds completed to June 2023. ~£11 million debt recovered. Recovery via payment arrangements and attachment of earnings based on affordability.
digital_economy_act_data_sharing.gcc_action: TODO — confirm whether GCC is enrolled in DEA data sharing. If not, consider engaging Cabinet Office.

implied_expectations_for_gcc.note: These are not legal requirements but represent the government's stated expectations and good practice standard against which GCC's service would be assessed.
expectations: Proactively identify and award discounts and exemptions — do not wait for residents to apply
Publish a formal vulnerability and hardship policy
Use soft reminders and signposting before statutory notices where appropriate
Offer affordable repayment plans before and after liability order stage
Do not automatically use enforcement agents against CTS claimants or severely mentally impaired persons
Require enforcement agents to refer vulnerability cases back to council
Maintain links with local debt advice services (Citizens Advice, National Debtline, MoneyHelper)
Apply section 13A hardship powers where appropriate for exceptional cases
Consider single view of debt approach to identify residents with multiple debts
Maintain in-year collection rate at or above 95.9% national average

calculation_basis.standard_bill: Based on at least 2 adults aged 18+ living in the property
calculation_basis.note: Bill does not increase if more than 2 adults live there
calculation_basis.discounts_available: Reductions available if fewer adults or certain circumstances apply

discounts.overview: Discounts reduce the Council Tax bill. Multiple discounts may apply but typically only the most beneficial one is awarded. Most discounts are statutory (national) but some are local discretionary policies.

legal_basis.legislation: Local Government Finance Act 1992, Schedule 1

eligibility.criteria: Only one person aged 18 or over lives in the property as their sole or main residence
eligibility.note: Some people are 'disregarded' and don't count as adults for Council Tax purposes

how_to_apply: Apply through My Gloucester online account
Download and email form to revenues@gloucester.gov.uk with 'Reference [Council Tax number]' in subject
Include Council Tax account reference number
application_process.processing: Continue paying until notified of decision
application_process.notification_requirement: Tell us within 21 days if circumstances change

change_in_circumstances.consequence_of_not_telling: May receive amended bill and penalty charge

items.name: Severely Mentally Impaired Discount
items.effect: Either 25% or 100% depending on household composition

legal_basis.legislation: Local Government Finance Act 1992, Schedule 1, Paragraph 2(7)

criteria: Person is severely mentally impaired (certified by doctor)
Person receives qualifying benefit
qualifying_benefits: Disability Living Allowance (middle or higher rate care component)
Universal Credit (limited capability for work element)
eligibility.evidence_required: Medical certificate from doctor confirming severe mental impairment

how_to_apply: Apply through My Gloucester online account
Download and email form to revenues@gloucester.gov.uk
Include medical certificate and benefit evidence

discount_levels.100_percent: If severely mentally impaired person is only adult, or all other adults are disregarded/also severely mentally impaired
discount_levels.25_percent: If severely mentally impaired person lives with one other non-disregarded adult
discount_levels.no_discount: If severely mentally impaired person lives with two or more other non-disregarded adults

legal_basis.legislation: Local Government Finance Act 1992, Section 13(1)(c) — discretionary reduction
legal_basis.note: Distinct from CTS which is made under s.13A(1)(a). Applied after statutory discounts, exemptions, disabled reduction and local CTS.

eligibility.age_history: Original scheme (April 2019) covered 18–21 only. Extended to 18–24 by Cabinet 10 January 2024, effective 1 April 2024.
eligibility.care_leaver_definition: Formerly a child in care of Gloucestershire County Council OR any other local authority in England, and became a 'Former Relevant Child' under Children (Leaving Care) Act 2000.
eligibility.residency: Must be resident and liable for Council Tax in Gloucester City Council area.
eligibility.liability_requirement: Must be the liable person — bill must be in their name. No discount where resident is in HMO and not the Council Tax payer.
eligibility.verification: Care leaver status confirmed by Gloucestershire County Council's 11-25 Permanency Service, who will seek confirmation from other local authorities where needed.
eligibility.geographic_scope: Applies to Former Relevant Children of any local authority in England (not limited to Gloucestershire CC — extended April 2024).

application_process.proactive_notification: GCC's 11-25 Permanency Service proactively provides names, addresses and dates of birth of qualifying care leavers.
application_process.self_application: Applications also accepted with verified care leaver status from 11-25 Permanency Service.
required_information: Details of other adults in property and relationship
Name of Leaving Care Worker (if known)
application_process.change_of_circumstances: Must notify within 21 days. Overpaid discount reclaimed through Council Tax account.
application_process.periodic_review: Revenues and Benefits Service undertakes periodic reviews appropriate to individual circumstances.

discount_calculation.application_order: Applied after statutory discounts, exemptions, disabled reduction and local Council Tax support.

end_of_discount.policy_change: May end if council policy revised

scheme_history.original: April 2019 — ages 18–21, agreed by all 6 Gloucestershire billing authorities and GCC.
scheme_history.extension: April 2024 — extended to ages 18–24. Agreed December 2023, Cabinet approved 10 January 2024.

backdating.general_rule: Accepted to start of financial year or date liability began, up to 12 months prior to application.

appeals.written_challenge: Within 1 month of decision. Longer period allowed in exceptional circumstances.
appeals.legal_basis: Administered under Local Government Finance Act 1992, subject to statutory appeals process.

_source.title: Council Tax Discount Scheme for Care Leavers — April 2024 (Appendix 2)
_source.note: Supersedes earlier version dated April 2019. Key changes: age extended to 24; geographic scope extended to care leavers of any English LA.

items.name: Carer Disregard
items.effect: Carer is disregarded - may result in 25% discount if only one other adult

legal_basis.legislation: Local Government Finance Act 1992, Schedule 1, Paragraph 2(5)

criteria: Provides care for at least 35 hours per week
Person cared for receives qualifying benefit
Person cared for is NOT your spouse, partner, or child under 18
qualifying_benefits_for_cared_person: Disability Living Allowance (middle or higher rate care component)
exclusions: Paid carers employed by care company (counted as normal adults)

how_to_apply: Apply through My Gloucester online account
Download and email form to revenues@gloucester.gov.uk
Evidence of care arrangement and benefits required

how_disregard_works.effect: Carer is not counted when calculating number of adults
how_disregard_works.example: If only carer and cared-for person live in property, and cared-for person also qualifies as disregarded, 100% discount may apply

items.name: Full-time Student Disregard
items.effect: Students disregarded - 100% discount if all adults are students, otherwise may result in 25% if one student with one other adult

legal_basis.legislation: Local Government Finance Act 1992, Schedule 1, Paragraph 2(4)

criteria: Enrolled on full-time course of education
Course lasts at least one academic year
At least 24 weeks per year
At least 21 hours study per week during term time
applies_to: Student nurses on courses leading to registration
Students under 20 in full-time further education
eligibility.exemption: Property occupied only by full-time students is exempt (Class N)

how_to_apply: Apply through My Gloucester online account
Download and email form to revenues@gloucester.gov.uk with student certificate

items.name: Apprentice and Youth Trainee Disregard
items.effect: Disregarded - may result in discount depending on household

legal_basis.legislation: Local Government Finance Act 1992, Schedule 1, Paragraph 2(6)

eligibility.apprentices: Undertaking apprenticeship under Apprenticeships, Skills, Children and Learning Act 2009

how_to_apply: Apply through My Gloucester online account
Download and email form to revenues@gloucester.gov.uk with apprenticeship agreement

legal_basis.legislation: Local Government Finance Act 1992, Schedule 1

eligibility.criteria: Left school or college after 30 April, aged 18-19
eligibility.disregard_period: Not counted for Council Tax until 1 November of same year
eligibility.child_benefit: If over 18 and someone receives child benefit for you, also disregarded

how_to_apply: Apply through My Gloucester online account
Download and email form to revenues@gloucester.gov.uk

items.name: Disabled Band Reduction
items.effect: One band lower (Band A gets 1/9th reduction)

legal_basis.legislation: Local Government Finance Act 1992, Section 13(2) and Schedule 1A

criteria: Property is main home of disabled person (adult or child)
Property has been adapted or has features essential for disabled person's needs
qualifying_features: Additional bathroom or kitchen for disabled person's use
Room (not bathroom/kitchen/toilet) used by disabled person for special purpose (e.g. dialysis)
Extra space inside property for wheelchair use
eligibility.assessment: Site visit required to verify features meet criteria

how_to_apply: Apply through My Gloucester online account
Download and email form to revenues@gloucester.gov.uk
Post form to: Gloucester City Revenues & Benefits, Eastgate Management Suite, Eastgate Street, Gloucester, GL1 1PA
application_process.assessment: Council officer will visit to verify eligibility

how_reduction_works.bands_b_to_h: Pay one band lower (Band D pays Band C rate)
how_reduction_works.band_a: 17% reduction on Band A charge (approximately 1/9th reduction)
how_reduction_works.note: This is not a discount but a reduction in the valuation band used for calculation

legal_basis.legislation: Local Government Finance Act 1992, Schedule 1A, Paragraph 4
legal_basis.status: Local discretionary discount (councils can set 0-100% for up to 12 months)
legal_basis.policy: Council decision on empty property discount

eligibility.criteria: Property is unoccupied and unfurnished
eligibility.discount_period: 25% discount for maximum 6 months from date furniture removed

property_based_discount.note: Discount awarded to property, not person
property_based_discount.consequence: If you buy/rent property already empty, you only get remaining period of 6-month maximum

long_term_empty.after_1_year: Empty homes premium applies (see property premiums section)

legal_basis.legislation: Local Government Finance Act 1992, Schedule 1A, Paragraph 4
legal_basis.policy: Council decision on major repair discount

eligibility.criteria: Property unoccupied and undergoing major structural alteration or repair to make it habitable
qualifying_works: Major repairs to roof structure (not just re-tiling/re-slating)
Total gutting including removing most internal walls (not stud/partition walls)
Making habitable after flood, fire, or subsidence damage
eligibility.once_per_property: Can only claim once per property unless new major damage (flood/fire/subsidence) occurs

application_process.evidence_required: Details of major works being undertaken

legal_basis.legislation: Local Government Finance Act 1992, Schedule 1A

criteria: Annexe occupied by relative of council tax payer of main property, OR
Annexe used as part of main property (not separately let)
eligibility.exemption_exists: Annexes occupied by elderly or disabled relatives may be exempt instead of discounted

legal_basis.legislation: Local Government Finance Act 1992, Schedule 1A

criteria: Employer requires you to live in property (job-related accommodation)
You are also liable for Council Tax on a second furnished property
eligibility.note: Applies to the furnished second home, not the job-related property

application_process.evidence_required: Proof of employment requirement and dual liability

items.name: Hospital/Care Patients
items.effect: Disregarded - not counted as adults for Council Tax

legal_basis.legislation: Local Government Finance Act 1992, Schedule 1, Paragraph 2

criteria: Long-term hospital patients
Residential care/nursing home residents
Hospice residents
Hostel residents (providing high level of care)

items.name: Persons in Detention
items.effect: Disregarded - not counted as adults for Council Tax

legal_basis.legislation: Local Government Finance Act 1992, Schedule 1, Paragraph 2

criteria: Persons in prison
Persons on remand
Persons detained under Mental Health Act 1983

items.name: Members of Religious Communities
items.effect: Disregarded - not counted as adults for Council Tax

legal_basis.legislation: Local Government Finance Act 1992, Schedule 1, Paragraph 2

eligibility.criteria: Members of religious communities whose principal occupation is prayer, contemplation, education, or relief of suffering

items.name: Foreign Spouses/Dependants of Students
items.effect: Disregarded - not counted as adults for Council Tax

legal_basis.legislation: Local Government Finance Act 1992, Schedule 1, Paragraph 2

eligibility.criteria: Non-British spouses/dependants of students prevented by Home Office from working or claiming benefits

property_premiums.overview: Premiums add extra charges to the Council Tax bill to incentivise bringing empty properties into use and discouraging second homes. These changes took effect from 1 April 2025 following the Levelling-up and Regeneration Act 2023.

legal_basis.legislation: Local Government Finance Act 1992, Sections 11B and 11C (as amended by Levelling-up and Regeneration Act 2023)

empty_homes_premium.what_it_is: Additional charge on top of standard Council Tax to encourage owners to bring empty properties back into use

1_to_2_years.empty_period: Empty and unfurnished for 1 year or more (from 1 April 2025)
1_to_2_years.note: Previously applied after 2 years - reduced to 1 year from April 2025

2_to_5_years.empty_period: Empty and unfurnished for 2 years or more

5_to_10_years.empty_period: Empty and unfurnished for 5 years or more

10_plus_years.empty_period: Empty and unfurnished for 10 years or more (from 1 April 2021)

property_based_charge.applies_to_property: Premium applies to property, not owner
property_based_charge.change_of_ownership: Buying property already empty means you inherit the premium
property_based_charge.removal: Premium only removed by bringing property back into use or qualifying for exception

qualifying_exceptions: Property where occupation is prohibited by planning restrictions
Property held by trustee in bankruptcy

second_homes_premium.what_it_is: Additional 100% charge on furnished properties not occupied as anyone's sole or main residence

premium_rate.applies_to: Properties which are furnished but not occupied as sole or main residence

exemptions.overview: Exemptions mean no Council Tax is payable for the period the exemption applies. All classes are defined in the Council Tax (Exempt Dwellings) Order 1992 (SI 1992/558) as amended. Some classes are time-limited; others continue indefinitely while circumstances are met. Residents must notify the Council of any change in circumstances.

items.name: Death of liable person (deceased estate)

eligibility.criteria: Property unoccupied since death of liable person. No other joint owners or tenants. Probate/letters of administration not yet granted, or granted within last 6 months.

items.duration: Indefinite until probate granted, then up to 6 months after grant

legal_basis.legislation: Council Tax (Exempt Dwellings) Order 1992 (SI 1992/558), Article 3, as amended

items.note: Where there are other residents, no exemption applies. Death of one joint owner does not trigger Class F.

items.name: Repossessed property

eligibility.criteria: Mortgagee (e.g. building society) has taken possession of unoccupied property under mortgage terms

legal_basis.legislation: Council Tax (Exempt Dwellings) Order 1992 (SI 1992/558), Article 3, as amended

items.note: Property must be unoccupied. Previously in schema as Class B — corrected: Class B is charity-owned empty property.

eligibility.criteria: Property owned by a charity, unoccupied, last used for charitable purposes

legal_basis.legislation: Council Tax (Exempt Dwellings) Order 1992 (SI 1992/558), Article 3, as amended

items.name: Last occupier receiving care (not in care home)

eligibility.criteria: Unoccupied — last occupier has moved elsewhere (not a care home or hospital) to receive personal care due to old age, disablement, illness, alcohol or drug dependence, or mental disorder

legal_basis.legislation: Council Tax (Exempt Dwellings) Order 1992 (SI 1992/558), Article 3, as amended

items.name: Last occupier now in care home or hospital

eligibility.criteria: Unoccupied — last occupier's sole or main residence is now a hospital, care home or nursing home where they are receiving care

legal_basis.legislation: Council Tax (Exempt Dwellings) Order 1992 (SI 1992/558), Article 3, as amended

eligibility.criteria: Unoccupied — last occupier has moved elsewhere to provide personal care to another person due to old age, disablement, illness, alcohol or drug dependence, or mental disorder

legal_basis.legislation: Council Tax (Exempt Dwellings) Order 1992 (SI 1992/558), Article 3, as amended

items.name: Last occupier in prison or detention

eligibility.criteria: Unoccupied — liable person is detained in prison, hospital by court order, or immigration detention. Does not apply if detained for non-payment of council tax or a fine.

legal_basis.legislation: Council Tax (Exempt Dwellings) Order 1992 (SI 1992/558), Article 3, as amended

eligibility.criteria: Unoccupied — last occupier is a student and has been a student for the whole period since leaving, or became a student within 6 weeks of leaving

legal_basis.legislation: Council Tax (Exempt Dwellings) Order 1992 (SI 1992/558), Article 3, as amended

eligibility.criteria: Unoccupied — occupation prohibited by law or by action of a public body (e.g. closing order, demolition order, planning condition)

legal_basis.legislation: Council Tax (Exempt Dwellings) Order 1992 (SI 1992/558), Article 3, as amended

items.name: Awaiting occupation by minister of religion

eligibility.criteria: Unoccupied dwelling held for occupation by a minister of religion as a residence from which to perform their duties

legal_basis.legislation: Council Tax (Exempt Dwellings) Order 1992 (SI 1992/558), Article 3, as amended

eligibility.criteria: Living accommodation owned by the Secretary of State for Defence, held for the purposes of armed forces accommodation. Applies whether occupied or unoccupied.

legal_basis.legislation: Council Tax (Exempt Dwellings) Order 1992 (SI 1992/558), Article 3, as amended

items.name: Trustee in bankruptcy

eligibility.criteria: Unoccupied — property is in the hands of a qualifying person in their capacity as Trustee in Bankruptcy under the Insolvency Act 1986 or Bankruptcy Act 1914. Applies even if jointly owned.

legal_basis.legislation: Council Tax (Exempt Dwellings) Order 1992 (SI 1992/558), Article 3, as amended

items.note: Does not cover unoccupied dwellings where the liable person is a company in liquidation

items.name: Annexe that cannot be let separately

eligibility.criteria: Unoccupied annexe forming part of a single property that includes another dwelling, which cannot be let separately without a breach of planning control (s.171A Town and Country Planning Act 1990)

items.duration: While unoccupied and planning restriction continues

legal_basis.legislation: Council Tax (Exempt Dwellings) Order 1992 (SI 1992/558), Article 3, as amended

items.note: Exemption applies whether furnished or unfurnished

items.name: All residents are full-time students

eligibility.criteria: Property occupied only by full-time students (and/or student nurses, foreign spouses/dependants of students, and school/college leavers). Also applies during vacation where students hold a tenancy and intend to return.

items.duration: While all occupiers qualify as students

legal_basis.legislation: Council Tax (Exempt Dwellings) Order 1992 (SI 1992/558), Article 3, as amended

eligibility.criteria: Property occupied only by persons under the age of 18. Owner/landlord name appears on the bill as persons under 18 cannot be liable.

legal_basis.legislation: Council Tax (Exempt Dwellings) Order 1992 (SI 1992/558), Article 3, as amended

items.name: Annexe occupied by dependent relative

eligibility.criteria: Annexe forming part of a single property, occupied by a dependent relative of the resident in the main dwelling. Dependent relative = aged 65+, or substantially and permanently disabled, or severely mentally impaired.

legal_basis.legislation: Council Tax (Exempt Dwellings) Order 1992 (SI 1992/558), Article 3, as amended

items.note: Annexe defined as a dwelling forming part of a single property that cannot be let separately without breach of planning controls

items.name: Severely mentally impaired only

eligibility.criteria: Property occupied only by person(s) who are severely mentally impaired and would otherwise be liable for council tax. Exemption also applies where occupied jointly by severely mentally impaired persons and full-time students.

legal_basis.legislation: Council Tax (Exempt Dwellings) Order 1992 (SI 1992/558), Article 3, as amended

items.note: Liable person must be severely mentally impaired. If someone else is liable (e.g. HMO or care home), exemption does not apply. Doctor certification and qualifying benefit required.

eligibility.criteria: Property occupied by a person with a relevant association with a visiting force from a country to which the Visiting Forces Act 1952 applies, or their non-British dependants

legal_basis.legislation: Council Tax (Exempt Dwellings) Order 1992 (SI 1992/558), Article 3, as amended; Visiting Forces Act 1952, s.12

eligibility.criteria: Property where a person who would otherwise be liable is a non-British diplomat or member of an international organisation headquartered in the UK

legal_basis.legislation: Council Tax (Exempt Dwellings) Order 1992 (SI 1992/558), Article 3, as amended

eligibility.criteria: Accommodation owned or managed by a prescribed educational establishment and used as student accommodation

legal_basis.legislation: Council Tax (Exempt Dwellings) Order 1992 (SI 1992/558), Article 3, as amended

eligibility.criteria: Caravan pitch not occupied by a caravan, or boat mooring not occupied by a boat. A pitch or mooring is a dwelling only where used as a person's sole or main residence.

legal_basis.legislation: Council Tax (Exempt Dwellings) Order 1992 (SI 1992/558), Article 3, as amended

legislation.regulations: Council Tax Reduction Schemes (Prescribed Requirements) (England) Regulations 2012
legislation.purpose: Prescribes scheme for pension-age claimants (national scheme)

local_policy.scope: Working-age scheme is locally determined by each billing authority

payment_date_change.description: Residents can change their monthly payment date to align with their pay date.

instalment_spread.description: Residents can spread instalments over the full financial year (12 months) rather than the default 10, or switch from monthly to weekly payments.

pre_summons.description: A negotiated replacement instalment plan for residents who have lost their statutory right to pay by instalments due to missed payments and are at risk of court action. Allows the resident to self-serve an offer to clear arrears before a summons is issued.

legislative_basis.note: The need for special arrangements arises from the Council Tax (Administration and Enforcement) Regulations 1992, which provides that the statutory right to pay by instalments is forfeited on default. The specific self-serve process is GCC discretionary policy, not a statutory requirement.

eligibility.note: The 31 March boundary is legislatively grounded — SI 1992/613 requires all instalments to be cleared within the relevant financial year.

pre_summons.how: Via My Gloucester account (self-serve online), with assisted support available through council contact channels where needed.
pre_summons.outcome_if_accepted: Revised bill issued confirming new amounts and payment dates.
pre_summons.outcome_if_rejected: Council notifies resident in writing or by email.

direct_debit_continuity.note: Direct Debit details held for a special arrangement are not used automatically for the following year's charges. Resident must set up a new Direct Debit via My Gloucester using the Change payment method or bank details form.

service_differentiator.note: GCC's self-serve online arrangement is more accessible than many councils, which require phone contact or submission of a financial circumstances form. Worth surfacing in chatbot and web content as a positive differentiator.

stage_coverage.pre_summons: Available where reminder, second reminder or final notice has been issued and no summons has yet been served.
stage_coverage.summons_liability_order: Modified SPA may still be considered with upfront payment and supporting income information, subject to policy conditions.

pre_summons.compassion_link: SPA is the main operational mechanism for residents who are engaging and need an affordable route to clear arrears before further escalation.
pre_summons.user_facing_summary: Before summons, this is the main route for catching up missed council tax payments and getting the account back on track.
pre_summons.customer_outcome: Catch up missed instalments before court action.
pre_summons.channel_summary: Available online through My Gloucester and can also be discussed through council contact channels.

post_summons.description: A council-managed arrangement available after summons or liability order stage where the resident provides financial information and, where required, an upfront payment.

available_options.note: Used as a last resort where a shorter arrangement is not viable but payment within the financial year remains achievable.

available_options.trigger: resident unable to pay within financial year
available_options.note: Used in exceptional circumstances and judged on individual merits.

requirements: Current employment or income details must be provided
Upfront payment required for standard post-summons arrangements
accepted_financial_evidence: Standard Financial Statement (SFS) prepared by an accredited organisation

previous_year_debt_rule.description: A smaller arrangement can be agreed for previous year debt, with emphasis on the current year's charge.

post_summons.failure_condition: If no upfront payment is made, or employment or income details are not provided, recovery action continues.
post_summons.compassion_link: Supports the cannot_pay and engaging_but_struggling pathways while preserving staged recovery controls.
post_summons.user_facing_summary: After summons or liability order stage, a special payment arrangement may still help a resident catch up missed payments, but it becomes a council-managed assessed arrangement.
post_summons.channel_summary: Handled through council-managed contact and assessment routes rather than simple self-serve setup.

special_payment_arrangement.user_facing_purpose: How to catch up a missed council tax payment.
special_payment_arrangement.plain_english_summary: If a resident has missed a council tax instalment, a special payment arrangement may let them catch up what they owe.
user_facing_when_to_use: You have missed a council tax payment
You have received a reminder or final notice
You want to catch up before the case moves further into recovery

who_pays.hierarchy: Liability determined by Local Government Finance Act 1992, Part 1, Chapter 1, Sections 6-9
who_pays.joint_liability: If multiple people at same level of hierarchy, they are jointly and severally liable (each responsible for full amount)

special_cases.hmo: Houses in Multiple Occupation - owner liable, not tenants
special_cases.care_homes: Resident liable unless meets exemption criteria

_source.reference: Local Government Finance Act 1992, Part I, ss.6-9

enforcement_policy.principle: Council Tax is a priority debt. Prompt recovery action is taken as unpaid Council Tax reduces funding for essential local services.
enforcement_policy.hardship_first: Residents struggling to pay should contact the Council as soon as possible. Support options (CTS, payment arrangements, hardship fund) are explored before escalation.
enforcement_policy.scam_warning: Beware of scam emails claiming arrears and asking to click a link. The Council will never ask for bank details via email or ask to verify them through a link. Check account status via My Gloucester only.

escalation_stages.name: Reminder notice
escalation_stages.required_response: Bring account up to date within 7 days AND continue with instalments
escalation_stages.note: Partial payment does not stop recovery action

escalation_stages.name: Second reminder notice
escalation_stages.required_response: Bring account up to date within 7 days AND continue with instalments
escalation_stages.note: If further instalments missed, no further reminder notices will be issued

escalation_stages.name: Final notice
escalation_stages.trigger: Failure to bring account up to date within 7 days of second reminder, OR further missed payment in same financial year

escalation_stages.name: Liability Order
escalation_stages.trigger: Full outstanding amount not paid, OR no payment arrangement agreed
escalation_stages.action: Application to Magistrates Court for Liability Order
escalation_stages.what_it_is: Court demand for the full amount of Council Tax owed plus costs incurred
escalation_stages.note: Do not ignore a Liability Order — further enforcement action will follow

post_liability_order_powers.overview: A Liability Order grants additional recovery powers. The Council will use these proportionately.

enforcement_agent.name: Enforcement agent (bailiffs)
enforcement_agent.description: Enforcement agents instructed to recover money owed plus their additional costs
enforcement_agent.powers: Can take goods belonging to the debtor to cover costs

attachment_of_earnings.name: Deductions from earnings
attachment_of_earnings.description: Employer legally required to deduct money from salary each month and pay directly to Council
attachment_of_earnings.basis: Amount depends on earnings level. Continues until full amount paid.
attachment_of_earnings.employer_obligation: This is a legal order — employer must comply

attachment_of_benefits.name: Deductions from benefits
attachment_of_benefits.description: DWP instructed to take money from eligible benefits and pay directly to Council

charging_order.name: Charging order
charging_order.description: Order means Council Tax debt can be claimed from property equity when property is sold (after mortgage paid)
charging_order.additional_power: Council may be able to insist on sale of property

bankruptcy.name: Bankruptcy
bankruptcy.threshold: Liability Order granted AND debt over £5,000
process: Statutory demand issued — full amount to be paid within 21 days
If unpaid, bankruptcy petition submitted to court
If granted: possible loss of home, bank accounts may be frozen, credit affected, official receiver investigates affairs
bankruptcy.note: Official receiver may control spending for a considerable period

committal.name: Committal (prison)
committal.process: Application to Magistrates Court for committal hearing
committal.important: If committal summons received, contact Council immediately and seek legal advice. Paying in full before hearing means court attendance not required. Failure to attend summons may result in warrant for arrest.

national_guidance_reference.breathing_space: Statutory breathing space scheme: up to 60 days protection from most enforcement for those in problem debt receiving debt advice. Mental health crisis moratorium: duration of crisis treatment plus 30 days. See: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/debt-respite-scheme-breathing-space-guidance/debt-respite-scheme-breathing-space-guidance-for-creditors
national_guidance_reference.enforcement_conduct_board: GCC should only instruct ECB-accredited enforcement agents. ECB accreditation covers 95%+ of enforcement volume in England and Wales.
national_guidance_reference.vulnerability_principle: Where vulnerability is identified at any stage — including by enforcement agents — the case should be reviewed. Agents must have contractual obligation to refer vulnerability cases back to the council.
national_guidance_reference.committal_in_practice: No one has been imprisoned as a main offence for council tax non-payment since 2018. Courts must find wilful refusal or culpable neglect. Committal proceedings are primarily a tool for engagement, not punishment.

vulnerability_hardship_policy.status: Resolved in v2.5.1 — internal Billing, Collection and Enforcement Policy July 2023 v1 confirmed
vulnerability_hardship_policy.why_this_matters: MHCLG best practice guidance (2021) expects billing authorities to have a formal recovery and enforcement policy that sets out how vulnerability cases are handled differently. The February 2024 government response to the LUHC Select Committee reinforced this expectation. Without a published policy, GCC's enforcement approach lacks the explicit vulnerability pathway that national guidance requires. This must be confirmed before any public-facing content is generated from this schema.
what_the_policy_should_cover: Definition of vulnerability as used by GCC (no national legal definition exists)
How vulnerability is identified at each stage of the recovery process
What different recovery pathway applies when vulnerability is identified
Conditions under which enforcement agent referral is suspended or not used
Approach to section 13A hardship reductions in exceptional circumstances
Signposting and referral process to debt advice services
How enforcement agents are contractually required to identify and refer vulnerability cases back to the council
Relationship between vulnerability policy and CTS claimants
references: LUHC Select Committee government response Feb 2024: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/council-tax-collection-government-response-to-the-select-committee-report/government-response-to-the-levelling-up-housing-and-communities-select-committee-report-on-council-tax-collection

billing_collection_enforcement_policy._section_note: Added v2.5.1. Source: GCC Billing, Collection and Enforcement Policy July 2023 v1 (internal document). Resolves all TODOs in vulnerability/hardship policy. Companion to Revenues & Benefits Sanction Policy August 2024.
billing_collection_enforcement_policy._document_status: internal_policy — not publicly indexed but formally adopted
billing_collection_enforcement_policy.guiding_principle: Fair, professional, consistent and timely recovery. Full consideration of individual circumstances to distinguish 'cannot pay' from 'will not pay'. Revenues Team preference is always to engage and find an affordable agreement.

conditions: All missed instalments must be cleared before any arrangement is agreed
Direct debit SPA must end within current financial year
Non-direct debit arrangements must be paid by the 15th of each month, within current financial year
pre_summons_stage.if_no_agreement: Summons notice must be served if debt cannot be cleared within current financial year

options: SPA over three months with upfront payment (four instalments total) — checked monthly
As last resort: full year arrangement with upfront payment
Income and expense form in exceptional circumstances if unable to pay within financial year — each case on merits
Standard Financial Statement (SFS) from accredited organisation accepted
conditions: Employment or income details must be provided to enter arrangement at this stage
If no upfront payment or income details available, recovery continues
summons_liability_order_stage.previous_year_debt: Smaller arrangement can be agreed, with emphasis on current year charge

vulnerability_framework.policy_statement: Cases classed as high vulnerability will be referred back from enforcement agents to the council. The council will take all reasonable steps to ensure inappropriate enforcement action is not taken against potentially vulnerable individuals.
vulnerable_indicators: Very confused or distressed, unable to understand basic information or make a decision
Long term or chronic illness (e.g. chronic asthma, cancer, MS)
Suffering from dementia or Alzheimer's disease
Household member requiring constant carer assistance
Claiming disability benefits or registered disabled
Change in circumstances (e.g. divorce, bereavement, job loss)
Severe financial difficulty and evidence of hardship
Households where there is evidence of potential abuse
vulnerability_framework.note: List is not exhaustive. Each case considered on its own merits. Low-vulnerability cases with ability to pay: arrangement made by enforcement agent, referred back to council only if arrangement is below normally expected level.

discretionary_hardship_scheme.description: GCC operates a council tax discretionary hardship scheme. Where a debtor applies and the scheme is agreed, the debt can be written off.
discretionary_hardship_scheme._publish_status: TODO — confirm current scheme criteria, application route, decision authority and fund status. Policy confirms existence but not operational detail.

write_off_triggers_council_tax: Debtor in financial hardship and debt is overpaid housing benefit or discretionary housing payment
Severe physical illness or mental condition where recovery would have detrimental effect
Debtor bankrupt or insolvent (except fraudulent overpayments)
Debt over six years old with no repayments and all recovery exhausted
bankruptcy_safeguards: Checks with social services to confirm debtor is not a known vulnerable person
All CTS records checked to ensure discounts and exemptions correctly applied
Written warning issued with signposting to advice agency and Insolvency Service booklet
Panel review: Revenues Service Delivery Manager, Revenues Service Development Manager, Council Tax and Billing Manager
billing_collection_enforcement_policy.charging_order_enforced_sale_safeguard: Written authorisation from Section 151 Officer required before enforcing sale. Full proportionality review required. Court makes final decision.

national_emergency_clause.description: In times of national emergency the council may make urgent adaptations including additional billing flexibility layers or suspension of enforcement action entirely.
national_emergency_clause.note: Used during COVID-19 period. Provides basis for future crisis response.

compassionate_recovery_principles._section_note: Added v2.5.2 — makes the policy's dignity, fairness and engagement-first stance explicit as a schema contract rather than only prose.
principles: Treat individuals consistently, fairly, with dignity and courtesy
Encourage early contact where the resident disagrees with a bill or has difficulty paying
Consider the resident's individual circumstances and ability to pay before choosing recovery action
Actively distinguish inability to pay from unwillingness to pay
Prefer affordable engagement and support before escalation where lawful and proportionate
Signpost discounts, exemptions, benefits, debt advice and hardship support where relevant

recovery_decision_framework._section_note: Added v2.5.2 — operationalises the policy distinction between residents who cannot pay and those who will not pay.

cannot_pay: income and expenditure shows insufficient disposable income
qualifies for means-tested support or debt advice intervention
vulnerable: meets one or more vulnerability indicators in vulnerability_framework
mental health crisis or breathing space protections apply
non_engaging: repeated failure to respond or provide required information
will_not_pay: resident refuses meaningful engagement or repeatedly defaults without reasonable explanation

recovery_decision_framework.override_rule: Where vulnerability or hardship is evidenced, normal escalation may be paused, modified or referred back for council review.

holiday_lets_and_self_catering.overview: Holiday lets and self-catering accommodation occupy a unique position - they can be liable for either Council Tax OR Business Rates depending on usage. This is a critical boundary that affects whether second homes premium applies.

legal_basis.legislation: Non-Domestic Rating (Definition of Domestic Property) Order 1991 (as amended)
legal_basis.voa_authority: Valuation Office Agency determines whether property is domestic (Council Tax) or non-domestic (Business Rates)
legal_basis.changes_april_2023: New stricter eligibility rules from 1 April 2023 make it harder to qualify for Business Rates

criteria_all_must_be_met.rule: Available to let commercially for short periods totalling at least 140 days in previous 12 months AND in current 12 months
criteria_all_must_be_met.evidence: Marketing evidence, calendar availability, advertising on commercial platforms

criteria_all_must_be_met.rule: Actually let commercially for at least 70 days in previous 12 months
criteria_all_must_be_met.evidence: Booking records, receipts for rental income, occupancy data
criteria_all_must_be_met.counting_rule: Property occupied immediately before midnight counts as one day let

criteria_all_must_be_met.rule: Let on commercial basis with view to making profit
criteria_all_must_be_met.evidence: Market rate pricing, active marketing, business records
criteria_all_must_be_met.not_commercial: Letting only to friends/family at nominal rates, or purely for personal use

criteria_all_must_be_met.rule: Owner intends to make available for at least 140 days in following 12 months

exclusions_from_day_counts: Nights property closed for repair or refurbishment
Personal use by owner (counts toward availability but not actual lettings)

new_properties.initial_liability: Public Gloucester and GOV.UK pages do not set out a full new-property transition rule. Treat detailed switching logic as requiring VOA confirmation.
new_properties.public_position: A property that does not meet the qualifying rules will be liable for Council Tax.

existing_properties.public_position: Public sources confirm VOA can review whether properties meet the self-catering criteria, but detailed monitoring cadence and operational handling are not fully described on the checked public pages.

when_liable: Property does not meet 140/70 day Business Rates criteria
Property removed from Business Rates list by VOA for non-compliance
New property not yet qualified for Business Rates (first 140 days minimum)
Owner chooses not to pursue Business Rates assessment

if_furnished_not_main_residence.rate: 200% of standard Council Tax (100% premium) from 1 April 2025
if_furnished_not_main_residence.note: This is why failing Business Rates criteria is very expensive - you pay double Council Tax instead of Business Rates

if_unfurnished.charge: Standard Council Tax or empty homes premium depending on duration

liability_person.owner_liable: Owner liable for Council Tax on holiday let, not guests/tenants
liability_person.joint_owners: Joint and several liability if multiple owners

if_eligible.charge: Business Rates instead of Council Tax
if_eligible.assessment: Valuation Office Agency sets rateable value
if_eligible.billing: Gloucester City Council bills and collects Business Rates
if_eligible.note: Business Rates often lower than Council Tax for small properties

small_business_rate_relief.eligibility: If only letting one property AND rateable value under £15,000
small_business_rate_relief.relief: Up to 100% relief depending on rateable value
small_business_rate_relief.apply: Download form from council website or contact revenues@gloucester.gov.uk
small_business_rate_relief.note: This makes Business Rates very attractive if you can qualify - potentially zero rates

second_homes_vs_holiday_lets.second_home: Furnished property not let commercially - pays 200% Council Tax from April 2025
second_homes_vs_holiday_lets.holiday_let_qualifying: Meets 140/70 criteria - pays Business Rates (potentially with relief)
second_homes_vs_holiday_lets.holiday_let_not_qualifying: Does not meet 140/70 criteria - pays 200% Council Tax as second home
second_homes_vs_holiday_lets.financial_difference: Massive financial incentive to meet Business Rates criteria or make property main residence

policy_intent.government: Tighter rules from April 2023 to stop 'fake' holiday lets avoiding Council Tax
policy_intent.council: Second homes premium from April 2025 to discourage empty properties and encourage residential use
policy_intent.combined_effect: Properties must be genuinely commercially let OR occupied as main residence - middle ground very expensive

new_holiday_let: Check VOA and GOV.UK guidance on self-catering and holiday let accommodation
Keep evidence of availability, actual lettings and commercial operation
existing_holiday_let: Respond to VOA information requests if asked to evidence qualifying use
If the property does not meet the qualifying rules it becomes liable for Council Tax
stop_being_holiday_let: Review VOA and council liability position if the property stops qualifying as self-catering accommodation
If the property is domestic and furnished but not anyone’s sole or main residence, second-home premium rules may apply
application_process.note: Detailed transition timings, exact switch dates and application processing times were not confirmed on the checked public pages.

evidence_to_maintain: Marketing materials and listings (Airbnb, Booking.com, etc)
contact_and_evidence.annual_declaration: Possible operational practice, but not confirmed on the checked public Gloucester pages

scenario_1.situation: Own second home, let to friends/family occasionally at low rates
scenario_1.outcome: Not commercial letting - liable for Council Tax with 200% second homes premium

scenario_2.situation: Airbnb property, available 200 days, actually let 90 days commercially
scenario_2.outcome: Meets criteria - eligible for Business Rates (potentially with small business relief)

scenario_3.situation: Holiday let, available 140 days but only actually let 50 days
scenario_3.outcome: Fails 70-day letting test - liable for Council Tax with 200% second homes premium

scenario_4.situation: New holiday let, first 100 days, let for 60 days so far
scenario_4.outcome: Still on Council Tax (no premium yet as working toward qualification), will switch to Business Rates at Day 141 if hits 70 days

scenario_5.situation: Former holiday let stopped commercial letting
scenario_5.outcome: Must tell VOA - moves to Council Tax, if keep furnished and not main residence pays 200% as second home

holiday_lets_and_self_catering.gcc_published_position: Business rates are charged on most non-domestic properties. Holiday rental homes and guest houses are explicitly listed as rateable property types by GCC. From 1 April 2023, self-catering properties must meet both the 140-day availability and 70-day actual letting thresholds to remain liable for business rates. Properties that do not meet these rules become liable for Council Tax instead.
holiday_lets_and_self_catering.public_fact_boundary_note: Only the 140-day availability rule, 70-day actual letting rule, and Council Tax fallback if the rules are not met are treated as public-source-confirmed facts in this document.
